codeReview.summary: Minimal, correct change. The commit removes the conditional publish in update_clock(), making it always publish unconditionally — aligning it with update_cursor, update_selection, update_document_content, and build_editor_scene. All five VAL-CORE assertions are satisfied: (1) no early returns on buffer failure, (2) no retry/is_back_available/scene_pending code, (3) all incremental updates use acquire_copy()/publish(), (4) full rebuild uses acquire()/publish(), (5) MSG_SCENE_UPDATED sent after every publish via unconditional compositor_ch.send() in main.rs. The unsafe block in update_clock (reinterpreting ShapedGlyph as bytes) is sound — ShapedGlyph is #[repr(C)] with a compile-time 8-byte size assertion at scene/lib.rs:164. The only behavioral change is that update_clock now publishes even if update_data fails (length mismatch), which is harmless: the buffer is a valid copy of the latest frame, and mark_changed is not called so the compositor's damage tracker produces zero dirty rects.

issues.description: When update_data fails, publish() is called but no node is marked changed. This means MSG_SCENE_UPDATED is sent from main.rs but the compositor will find zero dirty rects and do a no-op render pass. This is semantically correct (no tearing, no corruption) but slightly wasteful — one unnecessary IPC message + one render backend call with empty damage. In practice, update_data only fails on length mismatch which should never happen for the clock (always 8 glyphs = 64 bytes). Acceptable tradeoff for code simplicity.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md Key Source Files table still references 'copy_front_to_back call sites' for scene_state.rs. This description is now stale — copy_front_to_back no longer exists anywhere in the codebase. The triple-buffer migration is complete.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md Key Architecture section still says 'currently double-buffered, changing to triple' for the Core→Compositor transport. The migration is complete — it should say 'triple-buffered'.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The os-worker skill's 'When to Use' section still references 'double-buffer protocol' (line: 'Scene graph types and double-buffer protocol'). The protocol is now triple-buffered. The skill should be updated to reflect the current architecture.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker's skillFeedback notes that the feature was mostly completed by the preceding triple-buffer-protocol worker, with only one small inconsistency remaining. The skill could benefit from guidance on handling features where a precondition worker has already done most of the work — the current procedure assumes significant implementation work per feature.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.suggestedChanges: 'the feature was almost entirely done by the previous worker (triple-buffer-protocol) — only one small inconsistency (conditional publish in update_clock) needed fixing'

summary: PASS. The commit (30bc053) makes a single targeted fix: removing the conditional publish in update_clock() so all five scene update methods now unconditionally follow the acquire→write→publish pattern. All VAL-CORE-001 through VAL-CORE-005 assertions are satisfied. No old double-buffer patterns (copy_front_to_back, is_back_available, scene_pending, retry, DoubleWriter, DoubleReader) remain in services/core/. MSG_SCENE_UPDATED is sent after every publish via the unconditional send at the end of the scene dispatch block in main.rs. The unsafe code is sound (repr(C) type with compile-time size assertion). One non-blocking observation: publishing without any changed nodes causes a harmless no-op render pass. Four shared state observations flagged — AGENTS.md and os-worker skill contain stale references to double-buffering.
